$comment: Five worked examples used as few-shot turns by every provider. Each entry is one {problem, cqm_json} pair. The CQM JSON values are taken verbatim from tests/instances/ — they are already schema-valid under cqm_v1.json. Update both places together if you change a fixture. The Max-Cut and Number Partitioning examples are intentionally encoded in the NATURAL unconstrained form (no indicator/auxiliary variables, no linearization constraints) to anchor the model toward QAOA-friendly encodings; without them the LLMs reliably emit inflated forms (Max-Cut → 13-variable / 28-constraint / 69-qubit; Number Partitioning → 9-variable / 2-constraint / 25-qubit) that get skipped by every QAOA tier. Number Partitioning uses the ``objective.offset`` field so the perfect-partition optimum reads as 0 despite the shifted quadratic body (see docs on objective.offset in cqm_v1.json).

examples.problem: I have a graph with 4 nodes labeled 0,1,2,3 and edges (0,1), (0,2), (1,3), (2,3). Partition the nodes into two groups to maximize the number of edges that cross between groups.

cqm_json.description: Max-Cut on a 4-node graph with edges (0,1), (0,2), (1,3), (2,3). Natural unconstrained binary encoding: each x_i ∈ {0,1} marks which side of the cut node i is on; for every edge (i,j) the contribution to the cut is x_i + x_j - 2*x_i*x_j (which is 1 when sides differ, 0 when they match). NO indicator variables for edges. NO constraints. The objective is a pure quadratic form, ideal for QAOA / annealers because the lowered BQM has exactly N qubits where N is the node count.

variables.name: x_0
variables.description: Node 0 group assignment: 0 = group A, 1 = group B

variables.name: x_1
variables.description: Node 1 group assignment: 0 = group A, 1 = group B

variables.name: x_2
variables.description: Node 2 group assignment: 0 = group A, 1 = group B

variables.name: x_3
variables.description: Node 3 group assignment: 0 = group A, 1 = group B

test_instance.description: Optimum cut value = 4. Bipartition {0, 3} vs {1, 2} crosses every edge.

examples.problem: I have a set of 4 positive numbers: [3, 1, 2, 4]. Split them into two groups so the absolute difference between the two group sums is as small as possible. Tell me which numbers go in each group.

cqm_json.description: Number Partitioning on [3, 1, 2, 4]. Natural unconstrained binary encoding: each x_i ∈ {0,1} marks the side (0 = group A, 1 = group B). Let S = sum of all numbers = 10. The signed imbalance is (S - 2·Σ n_i·x_i); the squared imbalance expands to a quadratic form over x_i with linear coefficient 4·n_i·(n_i − S) and off-diagonal quadratic coefficient 8·n_i·n_j, plus a constant term S² = 100 kept as ``objective.offset`` so the perfect-partition optimum reads as 0. NO auxiliary variables (no sum_A, sum_B, d, or absolute-value linearizations). NO constraints. Lowered BQM has exactly N qubits where N is the number count — ideal for QAOA and annealers.

test_instance.description: Optimum squared-imbalance = 0. Perfect split: Group A = {3, 2} (sum 5), Group B = {1, 4} (sum 5). Any bit-swap of both sides is equivalent.

examples.problem: I have 5 items with weights [2, 3, 4, 5, 9] and values [3, 4, 5, 8, 10]. My knapsack can hold at most 20 units of weight. Which items should I pack to maximize total value?

cqm_json.description: 0/1 Knapsack: 5 items with weights [2, 3, 4, 5, 9] and values [3, 4, 5, 8, 10], capacity 20.

test_instance.description: Brute-force optimum: pack items {0, 2, 3, 4} for total weight 20, total value 26. (Items 0+1+2+3+4 = weight 23 exceeds capacity.)

examples.problem: I need to cover four locations {a, b, c, d} using as few service teams as possible. Team S1 covers {a, b}, S2 covers {b, c}, S3 covers {c, d}, S4 covers {a, d}. Which teams should I send?

cqm_json.description: Set cover: cover universe {a, b, c, d} using minimum sets from S1={a,b}, S2={b,c}, S3={c,d}, S4={a,d}.

test_instance.description: Optimum: select {S1, S3} (or {S2, S4}) for cost 2 â€” both partitions cover all four elements with two sets.

examples.problem: Schedule 2 jobs through 2 machines (A then B). Job 1 takes (2, 1) time units on (A, B); Job 2 takes (1, 2). Each machine processes one job at a time. Find the start times that minimize the makespan.

cqm_json.description: Job-Shop Scheduling, 2 jobs x 2 machines (A,B in fixed order). Durations: J1=(2,1), J2=(1,2). Disjunctive big-M encoding with M=7. Optimum makespan = 4 (J2 first on both machines).

variables.name: start_1_A
variables.description: Start time of job 1 on machine A

variables.name: start_1_B
variables.description: Start time of job 1 on machine B

variables.name: start_2_A
variables.description: Start time of job 2 on machine A

variables.name: start_2_B
variables.description: Start time of job 2 on machine B

variables.name: y_1_2_A
variables.description: On machine A, job 1 precedes job 2

variables.name: y_1_2_B
variables.description: On machine B, job 1 precedes job 2

variables.name: makespan
variables.description: Time at which the last job finishes machine B

test_instance.description: Optimum makespan = 4. Run J2 on A at t=0, then J1 on A at t=1; on B, run J2 first (t=1..3) then J1 at t=3..4. Encoding has 7 variables and 8 constraints — small enough for ExactCQMSolver.
